name: Garnet Hill
short_description: BLM public land site near Ely offering excellent garnet collecting on gentle slopes at 7,011 feet elevation. Personal use rockhounding with no permits required.
description:
Garnet Hill sits on public BLM land in the high desert terrain of White Pine County, Nevada, offering rockhounders an accessible location to search for deep red almandine garnets and associated quartz minerals. The site sits at 7,011 feet elevation with gentle slopes that make collecting comfortable for most skill levels. The area benefits from Nevada's arid climate, which means minimal vegetation obscures the ground and exposes fresh material on the surface and in washes after seasonal weather events.

The garnets here formed during the Precambrian and early Paleozoic metamorphic events that shaped the Great Basin geology. As the region underwent regional metamorphism, garnets crystallized in metamorphic host rocks and now weathering from these parent rocks creates a steady supply of loose specimens. The quartz-rich country rock and feldspar minerals found alongside the garnets indicate a complex metamorphic history typical of central Nevada's mountain ranges.

Collectors should expect to find garnet specimens ranging from small 1/4-inch crystals to larger chunks suitable for tumbling or display. The gentle slope and open terrain mean you can cover ground efficiently, and morning light works best for spotting the characteristic deep red and black mineral fragments against the tan and gray soil. Most productive collecting occurs in and around shallow washes where water action concentrates heavier garnets, or on freshly exposed outcrops where erosion has revealed new material.

As a BLM site, this location is free to access and collect from year-round under recreational use guidelines. The lack of entry fees or permit requirements makes Garnet Hill an excellent choice for families and budget-conscious rockhounders exploring the Nevada mineral belt.
history: Garnet Hill's garnets originated during the Precambrian metamorphic events that affected the Great Basin region, with later Paleozoic metamorphic overprinting creating the complex mineralogy seen today. The garnets crystallized in metamorphic schist and gneiss country rock as regional heat and pressure transformed existing mineral assemblages, and modern weathering and erosion continue to expose fresh specimens on the surface.
tips: Search shallow washes and gullies first, as gravity concentrates heavier garnet specimens there. Use a 10x loupe to examine tan and gray soil for the characteristic deep red and black translucent garnet crystals, which often measure 1/4 to 1/2 inch across. Bring a small hammer and chisel to expose fresh material from exposed outcrops, but focus on loose specimens on the surface since commercial excavation is prohibited. Visit after winter snowmelt or occasional desert thunderstorms when fresh material has been uncovered.
terrain_notes: Elevation 7,011 feet with gentle slopes covered in sparse desert vegetation. Terrain is relatively open with minimal brush, allowing good visibility of the ground. Walking surface is a mix of sandy soil, small rocks, and outcrops. A short walk from parking gets you into productive collecting areas. Footing is generally stable on gentle slopes with minimal loose rock danger.
best_season: May - October. Higher elevation means the site is cooler than lower Nevada deserts, but winter snow can make access difficult. Late spring through early fall offers the best combination of comfortable temperatures and accessible terrain.
written_directions: From Ely, Nevada, take Nevada State Route 50 northeast approximately 25 miles toward the Great Basin National Park area. Look for BLM access points and informal pullouts along the route near the White Pine County foothills. Contact the local BLM Ely District office or check current Nevada BLM maps for specific access directions, as road conditions and access points can change seasonally.
road_conditions: Unpaved dirt road to access point. High-clearance passenger vehicles recommended but standard vehicles may access some areas. Check road conditions seasonally.
parking_notes: Informal pullouts and BLM parking areas typical for this region. Capacity limited. No developed facilities.
collection_rules: Personal use only. Maximum 25 pounds per day per Federal Land Policy and Management Act (FLPMA). No commercial collection allowed. No motorized excavation or mechanical collection equipment. Recreational hand tools such as hammers, chisels, and picks are permitted for exposing material.
rules:
Personal use only
Maximum 25 pounds per day per FLPMA
No commercial collection
No motorized excavation or mechanical equipment
Hand tools only for exposing material
Collect only what you can reasonably carry
nearest_services: Ely, Nevada has gas, food, and lodging approximately 25 miles south. No services at the site itself.
accessibility_notes: Gentle slope terrain accessible to most hikers and collectors. Unpaved dirt road to parking requires careful driving. Walking surface is mostly stable sandy soil and small rocks with good footing. No wheelchair access. Suitable for families with older children and those with moderate hiking ability.

faq.question: What kind of garnets can you find at Garnet Hill in Nevada?
faq.answer: Garnet Hill produces almandine garnets, the deep red to black variety commonly found in metamorphic rocks. Most specimens range from 1/4 inch to 1/2 inch across and are suitable for display, tumbling, or jewelry use. You will find both loose specimens on the surface and material that can be chiseled from exposed outcrops.

faq.question: Do you need a permit to rockhound at Garnet Hill, Nevada?
faq.answer: No permit is required. Garnet Hill sits on public BLM land managed under recreational use guidelines. You can collect free of charge as long as you follow the 25-pound-per-day personal use limit and hand-tool-only rule.

faq.question: What is the best season to find garnets at Garnet Hill near Ely?
faq.answer: May through October offers the best collecting season. Higher elevation (7,011 feet) keeps temperatures moderate during Nevada summer months. Winter snow can make access difficult, and spring snowmelt often uncovers fresh material on the surface.

faq.question: What tools should you bring to collect garnets at Garnet Hill?
faq.answer: A small hand hammer and cold chisel work best for exposing material from outcrops. A 10x loupe helps identify garnets in the tan and gray soil. A small collection bag and sturdy gloves are essential. Leave motorized equipment at home as it is prohibited on BLM recreational collection sites.

faq.question: Is Garnet Hill suitable for beginning rockhounders and families?
faq.answer: Yes. The gentle slope terrain, easy access, lack of permits, and abundance of surface specimens make Garnet Hill ideal for families and beginners. Children ages 6 and up can participate comfortably. The site requires no special skills or expensive equipment beyond basic hand tools.

faq.question: Where exactly should you search for garnets at Garnet Hill?
faq.answer: Focus on shallow washes and gullies where gravity concentrates heavier garnet specimens. Also check freshly exposed outcrops and areas where recent erosion has uncovered new material. Sunny morning light makes it easier to spot the deep red and translucent crystals against the pale soil.

meta_description: Find almandine garnets and quartz at Garnet Hill on Nevada BLM land near Ely. Free rockhounding, no permit required. Perfect for families.
